W3 builder (audit items 5/6/7), reporting to Rob. Repo /Users/amanigga/Desktop/TAMAZIA-REBUILD/tamazia-audit-engine, branch p3-evidence @ b44b106. READ FIRST: docs/p3-wave2-reports/COMPLETENESS-AUDIT-transcript.jsonl LAST result entry (the audit - your items are its top-10 #5/#6/#7), CONSTITUTION.md Rules 9/11/12, caution.md C-147/C-153/§7, GAPS.md rows deadline-hang + module-scope-state, llm/gate.js + llm/router.js (you build on them), docs/discovery/digest-research-llm-agents.md Part A (NLI grounding), tools/domain-gates/budget-caps.js (the AST-gate pattern to follow), tools/health-gate/check.js (acorn usage).

YOU OWN ONLY: llm/entailment.js (new), llm/evals/** (new), tools/domain-gates/deadline-audit.js (new), tools/no-module-state/** (new), .github/workflows/llm-eval.yml (new), fixtures eval/calibration-known-bad/fixtures/p3-gate-*.{js,json}. Do NOT touch breach/** (R3 working there), eval/e2e/** (R2), facts/** (RT-F fixer), eval/red-team|reference-set/**. Do not git commit.

BUILD:
1. GATE 3 - llm/entailment.js (charter-faithful implementation, Rule 12 gate 3): checkEntailment(claims, {llmCall, deadlineMs}) -> per-claim {claim, premise_source_id, verdict: entailment|neutral|contradiction, ok}: each atomic claim = hypothesis, its cited span = premise; the INJECTED llmCall runs an entailment prompt (build llm/prompts/entailment.js beside it); every response passes llm/gate.js validateResponse with a closed 3-value enum schema; anything not 'entailment' -> ok:false (the consumer demotes to needs_review); unparseable/timeout -> ok:false (abstain default). Deterministic shell, scripted-fake tests (hostile: neutral verdicts, garbage, timeout). Do NOT wire into breach/adjudicator (R3 owns it) - export cleanly and note the one-line wiring for Rob.
2. llm/evals/run.js + fixtures/ - the blocking precision + abstain-rate harness (PRD tool #9): runs a fixture suite of adjudication + entailment tasks through the deterministic shells with SCRIPTED providers (no network): known-breach tasks (must rule violation), known-clean tasks with tempting-but-wrong evidence (must abstain/pass - precision), curated-immunity tasks (jury must not veto), out-of-set + drift + garbage (gates must reject). Report precision + abstain-rate; exit 1 when precision < 1.0 on the seeded set or any immunity veto fires. --json mode. At least 12 fixtures across the classes.
3. .github/workflows/llm-eval.yml - runs node llm/evals/run.js on PR/push (npm ci + node, same shape as ci.yml steps; workflow YAML must parse - the repo's collect-workflows gate will check).
4. tools/domain-gates/deadline-audit.js (GAPS deadline-hang, Rule 9): acorn AST scan over evidence/** llm/** breach/** for external-call sites (fetch/spawn/injected call invocations awaited) not inside/under a raceWithDeadline/withDeadline wrapper or lacking a reachable deadline arg - heuristic is fine but FAIL-CLOSED on its own parse errors (exit 2), selfTest earning its zero + calibration fixture p3-gate-deadline-audit.js (a seeded undeadlined await the gate must catch). Keep false positives near zero: only flag KNOWN external-call identifiers (fetchFn, launchBrowser, llmCall, call, spawnSync with http) outside deadline wrappers.
5. tools/no-module-state/check.js (GAPS module-scope-state, C-153): acorn scan for MUTABLE module-scope state in evidence/** breach/** llm/** facts/**: top-level let/var holding non-constant values, module-scope mutation (identifier assignment/push at module scope reached from exported functions is the risky class - flag top-level let/var + exported-function writes to module-scope bindings). Frozen consts + require caches exempt. selfTest + fixture p3-gate-module-state.js.
6. Wire both new gates as sweep lanes ONLY IF tools/sweep/run.js LANES is not being edited by others (it is not in any current agent's scope): add two entries following the existing pattern. Also register your fixtures in eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js CALIBRATIONS (small additive entries - W2e's wiring session is over, the file is unowned right now).
EXIT: npm test green, lint green, node tools/sweep/run.js GREEN (your lanes included, zero findings on the current tree - if a lane finds REAL pre-existing violations in others' files, report them, set the lane to report-only threshold for now rather than breaking the tree, and flag to Rob), calibration --strict green with your entries, health-gate zero on your files, python3 -c yaml parse of your workflow. British English, no em dashes, no new deps. REPORT to Rob: files+lines, the entailment prompt contract, eval harness fixture classes + precision/abstain output, both gates' detection rules + what they found on the real tree, exit codes, the one-line adjudicator wiring for Gate 3, honest gaps.

- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init
- review
- security-review

content.content:
1	# CONSTITUTION: tamazia-audit-engine
2	
3	These are the mechanically enforced rules of this repository. Every rule exists because the old estate broke it and shipped a false legal claim, a phantom mint, or a confident zero. A rule with no enforcing gate is theatre, so every rule below names the CI gate or tool that enforces it. If a gate cannot fire, the rule does not exist: fix the gate before writing code that depends on the rule.
4	
5	Enforcement-file status: this repo is at P0, so some named enforcing files are the committed names later phases must land, not files in the tree today. The Part III gate map marks every file **live** or **planned (Pn)**; a prose "Enforced by" reference to a file not yet in the tree means the planned file in that map.
6	
7	This repository is PUBLIC. No secret, token, key, password or connection string may ever appear in any file. This document is not legal advice and the engine's output is not legal advice; a standing not-legal-advice line ships on every rendered audit.
8	
9	Sources: docs/PRD.md §3, docs/discovery/digest-research-llm-agents.md, docs/discovery/digest-research-compliance.md, docs/discovery/digest-claude-ledger-forensic.md, docs/discovery/digest-findings-bible.md.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## Part I: The constitutional rules
14	
15	### Rule 1: One door per fact
16	Every client-facing fact (legal name, jurisdiction, sector, regulator, fine, law name, host, element checklist) has exactly ONE producer module. Consumers read the fact; they never re-derive it. The renderer introduces no facts.
17	
18	**Why:** the old estate had 10 producers of jurisdiction, 8 of host, 6 of sector, 5 of regulator; the stale door is the one the client sees. This class shipped a P0 three times: the ghost jurisdiction, "Sector regulator" printed on 51% of the catalogue, and the £17.5M fine fixed in the DB that never reached the client because three code files kept their own copy (digest-findings-bible §B1).
19	
20	**Enforced by:** `tools/one-door/check.js` (blocking gate, ported from cowork-os PR #342) run inside the sweep (`.github/workflows/sweep.yml` and `tools/sweep/run.js`, wired into `.github/workflows/ci.yml`) on every PR; `tools/fact-lineage/check.js` asserts a single producer per fact and that the renderer imports facts only through the payload contract (activates fully when `payload/schema/facts-lineage.json` lands in P4).
21	
22	### Rule 2: Catalogue-only law facts
23	The compiled catalogue artifact is the ONLY source of law names, citations, fines, penalty bands, regulators and enforcement intel. A literal law name, fine amount or regulator string in engine or renderer code fails CI.
24	
25	**Why:** hand-maintained copies drifted for months: `FW_NAME_CAT` was a frozen 285-code snapshot rendering "Uae Newcode"; `fineRate` regexes applied wrong ceilings to non-fining bodies; the self-learning loop invented statutes with fake legislation.gov.uk URLs (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b64, b65, b97).
26	
27	**Enforced by:** `catalogue/lint/no-literal-law-facts.js` (blocking, scans engine + renderer source for law-name/fine/regulator literals outside `catalogue/`) in `.github/workflows/catalogue-lint.yml`; the render truth-pack in `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` rejects any rendered fine, regulator or law title not present in the compiled catalogue.
28	
29	### Rule 3: No artifact, no breach
30	A breach finding must carry a deterministic artifact: a verbatim quote string-matched to the crawled corpus, a captured network event, a register row, or a failing DOM node. No artifact means the finding cannot exist, whatever a regex or a model said.
31	
32	**Why:** every breach Tamazia ever sent before v23.0 was an unreviewed regex match no model or artifact ever verified; a bare-word regex over raw HTML accused a real criminal-defence law firm of being hacked ("sex discrimination", `<slot>`) (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b84, e-adjudicator). Pentest doctrine: "a finding with no reproduction steps cannot be trusted" (digest-research-compliance §D).
33	
34	**Enforced by:** `breach/verifiers/` is the only path from proposer to adjudicator, and the payload schema in `payload/schema/` makes the artifact fields mandatory (missing artifact = schema-invalid payload, mint refused); `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` re-matches every rendered quote against the stored corpus word by word in `.github/workflows/render-truth.yml`.
35	
36	### Rule 4: Every gate fails closed and is calibrated against known-bad input
37	A gate that errors, times out or receives malformed input must BLOCK, not pass. Every analyser and gate must demonstrably fail on a seeded bad fixture on every run; a gate that has never fired is assumed broken.
38	
39	**Why:** mandatory audit gates ran AFTER the R2 payload was persisted; `llmPreflight()` failed open; the jurisdiction stage was marked `ran` unconditionally; three evidence layers threw ReferenceErrors on their first line for two whole versions while a fail-open catch shipped every audit as if the model had read the breaches (digest-findings-bible F-0001; digest-claude-ledger-forensic b106).
40	
41	**Enforced by:** `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js` in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` feeds every gate a corpus of seeded bad inputs each run and fails CI if any gate passes one; `tools/swallow-gate/check.js` (AST-level, repo-wide, also in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`) blocks any catch block that swallows without recording a typed failure state.
42	
43	### Rule 5: Reachable or DORMANT
44	Every module is either provably reachable from the mint entry point or explicitly declared in `DORMANT.md` with a reason. Dead-but-green code is a lie waiting to ship.
45	
46	**Why:** 13 modules / 691 lines of correct, tested, cited legal logic were unreachable from the mint, including `citation-gate.js`, the gate built to make an unevidenced monetary claim impossible, which had never executed once; `statute-rag.js` was required for months and never called (digest-findings-bible §B6).
47	
48	**Enforced by:** madge + dependency-cruiser reachability walk in `.github/workflows/reachability.yml`, blocking: any module not reachable from `mint/` and not listed in `DORMANT.md` fails the PR; `tools/domain-gates/reachability.js` additionally asserts every ACTIVE catalogue rule routes to a scanner branch that can actually fire.
49	
50	### Rule 6: Below-confidence output is quarantined, never shipped
51	Anything the engine cannot assert with evidence at or above the confidence floor routes to quarantine (`needs-review` or `compliance_unassessed`), never into a shipped claim. Ambiguity defaults to withholding the accusation.
52	
53	**Why:** ambiguous LLM entailment verdicts ("Unclear, leaning no") matched neither branch and the finding stayed CONFIRMED with its fine; a foreign-language site got a fabricated 16-breach GDPR cascade because English regexes ran on a French corpus; unreadable bot-walled sites were asserted against (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b135, b1, b126).
54	
55	**Enforced by:** the adjudicator contract in `breach/adjudicator/` (verdict enum has no "maybe ships" branch: only violation / needs-review / pass, unparseable verdict = needs-review); `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js` includes ambiguous-verdict and non-English fixtures that must land in quarantine; the payload schema rejects findings whose confidence fields are below floor but whose state is `violation`.
56	
57	### Rule 7: A mint is done only when row + HTTP 200 + truth-pack all pass
58	"Minted" means: the DB row exists, the live URL answers 200, and the Playwright truth-pass proves the rendered words match the payload. Anything less is not done, whatever the queue says.
59	
60	**Why:** the old queue reported "done" on 1,004 phantom audits; `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` adopted stale rows while the queue said done; a transport failure masqueraded as an idempotent conflict and a row that was never written was hunted for days (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b102, b103; PRD §1).
61	
62	**Enforced by:** `mint/post-write-assertions.js` runs inside every mint worker (row read-back + live 200 + truth-pack invocation before status flips to done); `tools/mint-reconciler/reconcile.js` in `.github/workflows/mint-reconcile.yml` (scheduled) diffs queue vs audit_pages vs leads and alarms on any done-without-page.
63	
64	### Rule 8: Budgets are caps, never floors
65	Every page, token, retry and time budget is an upper bound. No `Math.max` floors, no minimum waits, no "at least N seconds".
66	
67	**Why:** the SPA-render tail carried a 45-second FLOOR via `Math.max`, so a few shells always cost 45s or more and the ~45s-any-website capability was lost; the measured fix (E-236) restored a 43s crawl to 6.9s with identical accuracy (digest-claude-ledger-forensic §d).
68	
69	**Enforced by:** `tools/domain-gates/budget-caps.js` (AST scan for floor patterns on budget variables) in `.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml`; the Speed specialist's per-stage latency report in `eval/speed-budget.js` fails CI if any stage's configured budget behaves as a floor on the golden set.
70	
71	### Rule 9: Every external step has a hard deadline
72	Every LLM call, browser step, register lookup and fetch is wrapped in a hard `Promise.race` deadline. A slow dependency degrades the mint; it never hangs it.
73	
74	**Why:** a stuck Chromium held a mint hostage for 752 seconds because the 30s goto timeout did not bound browser launch + networkidle; exhausted free LLM tiers burned 30s × 3 retries × 3 gate attempts waiting for answers that were never coming (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b115, §d E-238).
75	
76	**Enforced by:** `evidence/` and `llm/router.js` expose only deadline-wrapped call primitives (no raw fetch/browser call exports); `tools/domain-gates/deadline-audit.js` in `.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml` fails CI on any external call site not routed through a deadline wrapper; timeout behaviour is exercised by hang fixtures in `eval/calibration-known-bad/`.
77	
78	### Rule 10: Three-state findings, and confident voice must be EARNED
79	Every finding is exactly one of **violation / needs-review / pass** (the axe-core doctrine: anything not assertable with certainty degrades to needs-review, and every false positive is a bug). Voice policy (the punchier hybrid, Aman-decided 2026-07-16): the confident "evidenced on your live site" voice is reserved for register-verified facts and adjudicated findings carrying verbatim quotes; needs-review items render in observation language ("detected X; this may implicate Act §Y, penalty band Z"); a standing not-legal-advice line appears on every audit. No finding is ever phrased as an adjudicated legal conclusion.
80	
81	**Why:** binary verdicts forced the verifier to call the real Equality Act fabricated because it could not say "unverifiable"; low-confidence attachments rendered as hard breaches; invented enforcement filler printed when no curated intel existed (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b100, b59, b96; digest-research-compliance §A axe-core, lesson 15).
82	
83	**Enforced by:** the payload schema (`payload/schema/`) types `finding.state` as the closed three-value enum and ties voice tier to state plus evidence fields (confident-voice strings on a needs-review finding = schema-invalid); `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` asserts the not-legal-advice line is present and that no needs-review finding renders breach-voice phrasing; `eval/golden/run.js` locks voice regressions.
84	
85	### Rule 11: The LLM is extraction and selection only; it never authors a fact
86	The LLM may select among retrieved candidate spans, extract from supplied material, and adjudicate proposed findings as a FILTER (it can remove or downgrade, never invent). It never writes a law name, fine, citation, jurisdiction or breach into existence. All LLM output is structured (schema-enforced), logged in full, and every prompt/model change is gated on a golden set built at least 60% from real production failures. Temperature 0 is not determinism: log inputs, outputs and model build for every call.
87	
88	**Why:** commercial RAG-backed legal tools still hallucinate in 1 of 6 or more queries (Stanford RegLab); our own self-learning loop invented "Cookies... Regulations 2020" with three different fabricated URLs and resurrected the repealed Disability Discrimination Act 1995 (digest-research-llm-agents Part A; digest-claude-ledger-forensic b97).
89	
90	**Enforced by:** `llm/gate.js` (ported, proven live: deterministic rubrics, never model self-confidence, three strikes to deterministic fallback) wraps every call; the adjudicator's filter-only contract is tested against a hallucinated-id fixture in `llm/evals/run.js` (`.github/workflows/llm-eval.yml`, blocking on precision and abstain-rate); full call logging asserted by `eval/calibration-known-bad/` replay fixtures.
91	
92	### Rule 12: The five structural-impossibility LLM gates (fail-closed AND-chain)
93	Every LLM-touched claim must pass ALL five gates. They compose: gates 1 and 2 are deterministic set/string operations, so fabricated citations and fake quotes have escape probability zero, not merely small. Everything rejected routes to abstention; the failure mode is "refuses to answer", never "confidently wrong".
94	
95	**Gate 1: Retrieval-gated emission.** A claim may cite only a `source_id` present in the injected retrieval set; a citation to a non-retrieved authority is unrepresentable in the output schema.
96	*Why:* 13-21% of legal citations across five commercial systems were hallucinated (arXiv 2606.00898). *Enforced by:* structured-output schemas in `llm/prompts/` whose source_id fields are constrained to the injected candidate list, validated post-hoc by `llm/gate.js` (out-of-set id = score 0, reject).
97	
98	**Gate 2: Verbatim-quote exact re-match.** Every quoted span must survive a normalised exact-substring match against the cited document before the claim may exist.
99	*Why:* models paraphrase inside quotation marks and fabricate pincites (Stanford HAI); our corpus regex ran on raw HTML while the quote came from stripped text, so hit and quote disagreed (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b32). *Enforced by:* `breach/verifiers/quote-match.js` (deterministic, blocking, one detection surface per rule); re-verified at render by `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js`.
100	
101	**Gate 3: NLI entailment per claim.** Each atomic claim is the hypothesis, its cited span the premise; anything not labelled entailment is rejected.
102	*Why:* fluent-but-neutral sentences are the classic grounded hallucination (Nature 2024 semantic entropy; Google NLI research). *Enforced by:* the entailment step in `breach/adjudicator/` with its verdict rubric scored in `llm/gate.js`; calibration fixtures with known neutral/contradiction pairs in `llm/evals/` must be rejected every run.
103	
104	**Gate 4: Abstain-by-default confidence floor.** The default output is insufficient-evidence; a claim releases only above a calibrated floor, and abstention is always the cheaper path.
105	*Why:* forcing an answer on every query "causes confident mistakes and hallucinations" (SelectLLM); ambiguous verdicts defaulting to CONFIRMED kept fines on findings (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b135). *Enforced by:* the floor constant lives in `breach/adjudicator/` and is regression-locked by `llm/evals/run.js` abstain-rate gates; lowering it without updating the eval set fails CI.
106	
107	**Gate 5: Diverse jury with veto-to-reject.** P0/P1 findings require a quorum of genuinely independent model families; any single veto rejects; the jury may never veto curated catalogue facts (SECTOR_CORE / SECTOR_AGNOSTIC immunity).
108	*Why:* weak judges have high true-positive but very low true-negative rates, so a rejection is more trustworthy than an approval; our "dual-model" cross-check once hit the same provider twice and was not independent; the LLM was once allowed to veto the SRA off a solicitors' firm (digest-research-llm-agents Pattern 5; digest-claude-ledger-forensic b73, b91). *Enforced by:* `llm/router.js` quorum configuration asserts distinct provider families; immunity sets tested in `llm/evals/run.js`; forked verifier legs share one guarded code path (the b92 lesson) checked by `tools/one-door/check.js`.
109	
110	### Rule 13: Serves is not bound: the Tier A/B/C jurisdiction matrix
111	A jurisdiction attaches only on evidence, graded by tier: **Tier A** (register row, regulatory authorisation, registered office) scores 5; **Tier B** (office postcode, local phone, ccTLD/hreflang, local currency) scores 3; **Tier C** (prose mentions, "we serve clients in...") scores 1. Attachment requires one Tier A or two Tier B signals. Tier C NEVER attaches law; at most it feeds an advisory tier. Serving a market is not being bound by its law; law applicability is then a pure set-membership query over the applicability matrix (jurisdiction × sector × activity tags), and the verdict is graded with an evidence chain, KYB-style, never a boolean.
112	
113	**Why:** `/incorporated in/` anchored to no country judged 6 of 7 real UK/EU/UAE law-firm footers "established in the United States", and Mills & Reeve (UK) was served US ABA rules and ADA law, 4 of 8 findings jurisdictionally void; template hreflang pulled EU law onto a US clinic; a jurisdiction statement told a UAE firm the CCPA applied (digest-findings-bible DG-05; digest-claude-ledger-forensic b55, b128; digest-research-compliance §C).
114	
115	**Enforced by:** `facts/jurisdiction.js` is the single door for jurisdiction (Rule 1 gate applies); `tools/domain-gates/nexus-anchoring.js` in `.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml` replays the known-bad footer corpus (the Mills & Reeve class) every run; `eval/reference-set/run.js` fails on any jurisdiction contradiction against the hand-verified firms.
116	
117	### Rule 14: Provenance-mandatory catalogue rows
118	No catalogue row (law, rule, penalty, enforcement case) exists without provenance: source, official URL verified by FETCHING it and confirming the page IS that law (a 200 with matching content, never a 202 or an unfetched string), and last_synced. Discovered candidates are three-state (verified / rejected / unverifiable); only verified rows are promoted, and promotion of legal judgements is human-gated.
119	
120	**Why:** the official-URL "gate" checked only that a URL string was present; legislation.gov.uk answers 202-with-empty-body to ANY URL shape, so the status-code gate passed every fabrication; a full discovery run over 151 candidates yielded zero promotable laws (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b97, b98, b99).
121	
122	**Enforced by:** `catalogue/lint/provenance.js` and `catalogue/lint/citation-completeness.js` (blocking) in `.github/workflows/catalogue-lint.yml`: every row must carry resolvable provenance fields and every ACTIVE rule a citation; the catalogue compiler in `catalogue/` refuses to emit an artifact containing an unverified row; enforcement-intel population is human-gated per `catalogue/lint/enforcement-crossval.js`.
123	
124	---
125	
126	## Part II: Supplementary mechanical rules (earned by specific failures)
127	
128	### Rule 15: No scan cache; the engine version is load-bearing
129	A legal-evidence engine keeps no scan cache (a day-old scan dated today is not evidence). Any change to scan logic bumps ENGINE_VERSION, the version rides the idempotency key, and the DB-level trigger keyed on `engine_flags.required_engine_version` is the lock every minter must pass.
130	
131	**Why:** `scanner_cache` replayed pre-fix scans so "it's fixed now" was false four rounds running; a rogue pre-gate Hetzner cron minted on v19 for days; a partial catalogue (290 frameworks, 0 rules) was cached process-wide and connect attached nothing (digest-claude-ledger-forensic §c, b44, b107).
132	
133	**Enforced by:** `.github/workflows/engine-version-guard.yml` fails any PR touching scan logic without a version bump; the DB gate is ported as part of `mint/`; `tools/mint-reconciler/reconcile.js` alarms on any row minted under a stale version.
134	
135	### Rule 16: No secrets, ever (public repo)
136	Nothing matching a credential shape (ghp_*, npg_*, sk-*, cfut_*, xoxb-*, postgres://, or any key/token/password) may appear in any file, fixture, log capture or golden payload. Golden fixtures are structural-only and pseudonymised; raw payloads with prospect PII stay gitignored.
137	
138	**Why:** a leaked webhook secret persisted verbatim in a harvested review snapshot on the old estate (digest-findings-bible GR-0002/CR-0010); golden fixtures nearly leaked prospect PII into a public repo (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b122).
139	
140	**Enforced by:** `.github/workflows/secret-scan.yml` (gitleaks, blocking, full history on push) plus GitHub push protection; `eval/golden/` fixture linter rejects email addresses and person names outside the pseudonym dictionary.
141	
142	### Rule 17: Done means verified against ground truth
143	No agent, script or session reports a change done until verified at the end against ground truth: the git diff, the DB row, the live minted payload, the deployed asset. In-loop assumptions ("already present, skip") are not verification.
144	
145	**Why:** a 17-node sector insert was reported done three times while silently skipping; PR #114 on the website was an empty merge whose "deploy success" shipped nothing; a fix merged to one leg while the quorum leg kept the bug (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b117, b118, §c).
146	
147	**Enforced by:** Rule 7's mint reconciler for engine output; `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` for rendered output; CI status on the actual merge commit is the only accepted "done" signal per AGENTS.md Fleet Rule 1.
148	
149	---
150	
151	## Part III: The gate map (rule → enforcing file)
152	
153	**Legend:** files LIVE today: `tools/one-door/check.js`, `tools/swallow-gate/check.js`, `tools/fact-lineage/check.js`, `tools/sweep/*`, `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js`, `eval/golden/run.js`, `eval/reference-set/verify.js`, `payload/contract/index.js`, `payload/schema/payload.schema.json`, and workflows `ci.yml`, `sweep.yml`, `codeql.yml`, `semgrep.yml`. Every other file in this table is **PLANNED (P1+)**: it is the committed name the owning phase must land, and this table is the contract for it. A planned gate is not an excuse — the rule binds from day one; until its dedicated file lands, the closest live gate (sweep/ci) covers what it can and the rest is enforced in review.
154	
155	| Rule | Enforcing gate | File |
156	|---|---|---|
157	| 1 One door per fact | one-door + fact-lineage | `tools/one-door/check.js` (live, runs in ci/sweep), `tools/fact-lineage/check.js` (live, arms fully in P4) |
158	| 2 Catalogue-only law facts | catalogue lint + truth-pack | `catalogue/lint/no-literal-law-facts.js` (planned P2), `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` (planned P4); today `tools/one-door/check.js` (live) blocks fine/regulator/law-title literals outside `catalogue/` |
159	| 3 No artifact no breach | payload schema + verifiers | `payload/schema/payload.schema.json` (live), `breach/verifiers/` (planned P3), `.github/workflows/render-truth.yml` (planned P4) |
160	| 4 Fail-closed calibrated gates | known-bad corpus + silent-swallow AST | `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js` (live, in ci.yml), `tools/swallow-gate/check.js` (live, in ci.yml + sweep) |
161	| 5 Reachable-or-DORMANT | reachability walk | `DORMANT.md` (lands with the first module), reachability walk live in `tools/sweep/collect-local.js` (arms when `mint/worker.js` or `mint/index.js` exists); madge + dependency-cruiser live in ci.yml |
162	| 6 Quarantine below confidence | adjudicator contract + fixtures | `breach/adjudicator/` (planned P3), `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js` (live) |
163	| 7 Mint = row+200+truth-pack | post-write assertions + reconciler | `mint/post-write-assertions.js`, `tools/mint-reconciler/reconcile.js`, `.github/workflows/mint-reconcile.yml` (all planned P4) |
164	| 8 Budgets are caps | budget-caps gate + speed report | `tools/domain-gates/budget-caps.js`, `eval/speed-budget.js`, `.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml` (all planned P3) |
165	| 9 Hard external deadlines | deadline-audit gate | `tools/domain-gates/deadline-audit.js` (planned P3), hang fixtures in `eval/calibration-known-bad/` |
166	| 10 Three-state + earned voice | payload schema + truth-pack + golden | `payload/schema/payload.schema.json` (live), `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` (planned P4), `eval/golden/run.js` (live) |
167	| 11 LLM extraction/selection only | gate rubric + LLM eval harness | `llm/gate.js`, `llm/evals/run.js`, `.github/workflows/llm-eval.yml` (all planned P3) |
168	| 12 The 5 structural gates | schema constraint, quote-match, entailment, floor, quorum | `llm/prompts/` schemas, `breach/verifiers/quote-match.js`, `breach/adjudicator/`, `llm/router.js`, `llm/evals/run.js` (all planned P3) |
169	| 13 Tier A/B/C jurisdiction | nexus-anchoring + reference set | `facts/jurisdiction.js` (planned P2), `tools/domain-gates/nexus-anchoring.js` (planned P3), `eval/reference-set/verify.js` (live) |
170	| 14 Provenance-mandatory catalogue | provenance + citation lints | `catalogue/lint/provenance.js`, `catalogue/lint/citation-completeness.js` (all planned P2) |
171	| 15 No cache + version gate | engine-version guard + DB trigger | `.github/workflows/engine-version-guard.yml` (planned P4), `mint/` DB gate (planned P4) |
172	| 16 No secrets | secret scan + fixture linter | Semgrep p/secrets pack (live, `.github/workflows/semgrep.yml`); `.github/workflows/secret-scan.yml` gitleaks lane + `eval/golden/` fixture linter (planned P1) |
173	| 17 Done = ground truth | reconciler + truth-pack + CI status | `tools/mint-reconciler/reconcile.js`, `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` (planned P4); CI status on the merge commit (live) |
174

Either add the entailment step + neutral/contradiction fixtures, or record an explicit founder-approved divergence. Owner: Founder + LLM.

content.content:
=== digest Part A NLI-relevant lines ===
9:### The threat model you are designing against (why "unlikely" is not good enough)
21:| 3 | Verify each output sentence by **NLI entailment** against its cited premise (DeBERTa-v3-large/MNLI-class model, or a purpose-built verifier like Luna); keep only claims labeled *entailment*. | Trust fluency as a proxy for faithfulness; a well-formed sentence that is *neutral* w.r.t. the source is a hallucination. | [Nature semantic entropy](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-07421-0); [Luna, arXiv 2406.00975](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.00975); [Google, Sources of LLM Hallucination in NLI](https://research.google/pubs/sources-of-llm-hallucination-in-natural-language-inference/) |
24:| 6 | **Enforce structured outputs with constrained decoding** (OpenAI Strict mode / grammar-masked tokens), so every claim ships with mandatory fields: `source_id`, `char_span`, `verbatim_quote`, `entailment_label`. Missing field ⇒ cannot emit. | Rely on prompt-only "please return JSON." OpenAI reports ~100% schema-following with constrained decoding vs <40% with prompting. | [OpenAI Structured Outputs guide](https://logic.inc/resources/structured-outputs-guide); [JSONSchemaBench, arXiv 2501.10868](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.10868) |
25:| 7 | Use **schema to enforce shape, then a separate semantic gate** (NLI + quote match). Recognize the boundary: structured outputs guarantee *shape*, not *truth*. | Conflate "valid JSON" with "true claim." Schema conformance says nothing about semantic correctness. | [Aidan Cooper, Constrained Decoding](https://www.aidancooper.co.uk/constrained-decoding/); [Logic.inc](https://logic.inc/resources/structured-outputs-guide) |
32:### THE 5 PATTERNS THAT MAKE A FALSE LEGAL CLAIM *STRUCTURALLY IMPOSSIBLE* (not merely unlikely)
34:The distinction: an *unlikely* false claim is one a model was discouraged from making; a *structurally impossible* one is a claim the system **physically cannot emit** because a hard gate rejects it. Each pattern below is a gate that fails *closed*.
40:**Pattern 3 — NLI entailment gate per claim.** Each atomic claim is the *hypothesis*; its cited span is the *premise*; a fine-tuned entailment model (DeBERTa-MNLI class, or Luna-style eval model) must return `entailment` — `neutral` and `contradiction` are both rejected. Because the gate scores every sentence independently against its own source, an unsupported inference cannot pass even if the surrounding text is fluent ([Nature 2024](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-07421-0); [Luna](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.00975)). Caveat to design around: NLI models themselves hallucinate via memorization/lexical-overlap bias, so use a verifier NLI model *not* trained on your corpus and audit its calibration ([Google Research](https://research.google/pubs/sources-of-llm-hallucination-in-natural-language-inference/)).
42:**Pattern 4 — Abstain-by-default with a confidence floor (selective prediction).** The default output is "insufficient authority / escalate to counsel." A claim is released only if it clears (a) a retrieval-support threshold, (b) the NLI gate, and (c) a calibrated confidence floor. Coverage is deliberately traded for risk: the system answers the subset it is likely correct on and abstains elsewhere. Note the research nuance — *calibration alone is neither sufficient nor necessary* for good selective prediction, so combine confidence scoring + uncertainty estimation + explicit abstention rewards rather than relying on a single confidence number ([SelectLLM/OpenReview](https://openreview.net/forum?id=JJPAy8mvrQ); [I-CALM](https://arxiv.org/html/2604.03904v1); [Uncertainty-Aware Abstention w/ provable guarantees, arXiv 2607.04430](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.04430)).
46:**Why these five compose into structural impossibility:** they are *independent, fail-closed AND-gates*. A false claim must simultaneously (1) reference a retrieved source, (2) match a real verbatim quote, (3) be entailed by that source, (4) clear the confidence floor, and (5) survive unanimous jury approval. The probability of a false claim passing all five is the *product* of five independent escape probabilities — and because gates 1–2 are deterministic string/set operations (not probabilistic), whole classes of error (fabricated citations, fake quotes) have escape probability *zero*, not merely small. Everything the gates reject routes to abstention, so the failure mode is "refuses to answer," never "confidently wrong." The residual risk that remains — a claim that is genuinely supported by a real, correctly-quoted, entailing source but is nonetheless the *wrong* legal answer for the question — is a *retrieval/relevance* problem, addressed by Pattern 12 (hybrid retrieval) + the golden-set regression gate, and is the correct place to concentrate human review.
92:Both halves reduce to the same principle: **separate the fallible generator from an independent, fail-closed verifier, and define "acceptable output" as "passed the verifier," never "the model said so."** In A the verifier is a stack of deterministic gates (retrieval-set membership, exact quote match, NLI entailment, confidence floor, jury veto) whose composition drives the escape probability of a false legal claim toward zero. In B the verifier is CI + an adversarial critic agent whose green light is the only definition of "merged." The "Rob" orchestrator's real job is not to write code or claims — it is to *decompose work into units with pre-committed acceptance tests and route every unit through verification*. The single highest-leverage investment for the user is the **golden/eval set** (shared by both A and B): it is the ground truth that turns "the agents seem to be working" into "the fleet provably has not regressed," and it is, per the sources, "the single most important asset" — more important than model choice or framework.
94:Primary anchors: [Anthropic Building Effective Agents](https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents) · [Anthropic Multi-Agent Research System](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system) · [Claude Code best practices](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices) · [UC Berkeley MAST](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657) · [Stanford HAI legal hallucination study](https://hai.stanford.edu/news/ai-trial-legal-models-hallucinate-1-out-6-or-more-benchmarking-queries) · [Thinking Machines: Defeating Nondeterminism](https://thinkingmachines.ai/blog/defeating-nondeterminism-in-llm-inference/) · [Nature: semantic entropy](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-07421-0) · [OpenAI Structured Outputs](https://logic.inc/resources/structured-outputs-guide).

=== ARTIFACT_TYPES ===
'use strict';
/**
 * breach/artifact-types.js - THE one door for the breach artifact-type vocabulary (Constitution
 * Rule 1: one door per fact; Rule 3: no artifact, no breach). Every deterministic artifact a breach
 * candidate may carry has exactly one canonical `type` string, and it is DEFINED here and NOWHERE
 * else. The proposer (breach/proposers/), the verifier dispatcher (breach/verifiers/quote-match.js)
 * and the adjudicator's evidence-kind classifier (breach/adjudicator/evidence-kind.js) all IMPORT
 * this enum rather than re-declaring the literals; before this file existed each side kept its own
 * copy and they drifted (the proposer emitted `network_event`/`coverage_proof` while the adjudicator
 * still keyed on the old-estate `network_request`/`cookie_jar_entry`/`corpus_quote` literals, so a
 * real PECR observation reaching the adjudicator was silently quarantined - the C-084 disease
 * resurrected through a name mismatch).
 *
 * The CLOSED set (Rule 3 lists a verbatim quote, a captured network event, a register row, and a
 * failing DOM node; this repo's absence lane adds a coverage proof and a register-absence proof):
 *   quote             a verbatim quote string-matched to the crawled corpus (breach/verifiers/quote-match.js)
 *   network_event     a captured browser network/observation event (breach/verifiers/network-event.js)
 *   register_row      an EXACT public-register row the candidate cites as PRESENT (breach/verifiers/register-row.js)
 *   register_absence  a definitive register NO-MATCH: the lookup RAN and returned no row (breach/verifiers/register-absence.js)
 *   coverage_proof    proof the surface behind an ABSENCE claim was actually, sufficiently crawled (breach/verifiers/coverage-proof.js)
 *
 * Pure data: no I/O, no clock, no env, no law/fine/regulator literal (Rule 2).
 */

// ARTIFACT_TYPES: the canonical string for each artifact class. Consumers reference these constants,
// never the bare literal, so a rename is a one-line change here and CI (jscpd/one-door) has one door
// to police.
const ARTIFACT_TYPES = Object.freeze({
  QUOTE: 'quote',
  NETWORK_EVENT: 'network_event',
  REGISTER_ROW: 'register_row',
  REGISTER_ABSENCE: 'register_absence',
  COVERAGE_PROOF: 'coverage_proof',
});

// The closed membership set is kept MODULE-INTERNAL, not exported: Object.freeze() does not stop a
// Set's own .add()/.delete() (they mutate an internal slot, not a property), so an exported "frozen"
// Set would be a false promise of immutability. Callers get the genuinely-frozen ARTIFACT_TYPES
// object and the isArtifactType() guard instead; neither can be used to smuggle a new type in.
const ARTIFACT_TYPE_SET = new Set(Object.values(ARTIFACT_TYPES));

I'll implement a helper to extract that root call and check if its callee matches a known external pattern.

Now I'm refining the detection rule: a KNOWN external call counts if it's awaited (directly or through a promise chain), not wrapped in another call, and has no deadline argument. I'll track a `wrapped` flag during traversal to mark when we're inside a wrapper call's arguments, then apply this rule consistently. Refining the approach: instead of flagging during generic call traversal, I'll resolve the awaited call at the AwaitExpression node itself and mark it there. The walk needs to track `wrapped` more carefully—when descending into a wrapper's arguments, those descendants should be marked as wrapped, but the callee itself keeps the inherited wrapped state. For AwaitExpression nodes, I'll trace back to find the root external call being awaited and flag that directly. The spawn rule checks every call expression for HTTP spawning without a wrapper, flagging cases like synchronous curl commands. I'm also defining helper functions to detect whether a call has its own deadline argument and to identify known external callees by checking against a list of recognized identifiers and member properties. Writing fixture code... if it's an http URL string. So `spawnSync('curl', ['https://example.com'])` should trigger — 'curl' matches the command check and the array contains an http string. Let me verify my detection logic handles this correctly before finalizing the fixture.

Now I'm structuring the deadline-audit gate itself, starting with helper functions to identify known external callees and wrapper patterns. Now I need to handle the acorn dependency carefully—I'll conditionally require it like budget-caps does, using a FORCE_REFUSE environment variable and useAcorn flag so that if acorn isn't available, the scanner refuses to run and the self-test proves that refusal. The deadline-audit calibration is working perfectly—it caught all 4 seeded leaks in my fixture file and scanned 12 fixture files total without finding any issues in the others, which means there's no cross-contamination. Tests are passing and the linting is clean. Now I'm moving on to building the no-module-state gate using the design I've outlined.

I'm defining two detection classes: M1 flags accumulators (UpdateExpression, arithmetic/bitwise compound assignments, or mutating method calls on module-scope bindings inside functions), and M2 flags unguarded reassignments (plain `=` assignments to module-scope `let`/`var` bindings that aren't in a module-init IIFE and lack an early guard check). The approach involves parsing the code, collecting module-scope binding names from top-level VariableDeclarations (distinguishing between reassignable `let`/`var` and mutable `const` objects/arrays), and then checking for violations within function scopes while accounting for shadowing and destructuring patterns. not shadowed locally). To keep it simple and avoid false positives, I'll collect all locally-declared names anywhere within the function's subtree—including nested scopes—as "shadowed," which over-exempts slightly but errs on the safe side. Since the tree uses distinctive binding names that won't naturally shadow, and the fixture uses `_WARN`/`_SWARN` which also won't be shadowed, this precision trade-off won't affect the results.

For the implementation, I'll write a helper that collects a function's own local names by walking its body without descending into nested functions, then track a stack of enclosing functions during the write-detection walk to check if a name is shadowed by any enclosing scope. n Block. So I'm defining guardedBindings as the set of names that appear in top-level if-statements within the function body where the condition checks that name and the consequent contains a return, which lets me identify cached values or early exits that protect module bindings from being shadowed.

Now I need to detect mutations: UpdateExpressions where the argument is either a direct identifier N or a member expression rooted in N (like `_state.count++`), plus AssignmentExpressions with the `=` operator.

For assignments, the left side matters — if it's an identifier N, that's a binding reassignment (M2), but if it's a member expression on a module object like `_cache.x = v`, that's also a mutation of module state (M1). I need to be careful here: reassigning the binding itself (`_vocabCache = {...}`) is the guarded pattern I want to allow, but mutating properties on module objects is the leak I'm trying to catch. Compound assignment operators like `+=`, `-=`, etc. on a binding N count as M1 accumulators, though the logical variants (`||=`, `&&=`, `??=`) are idempotent and can be skipped. I also need to check for mutating method calls.

For member expressions where the root identifier N is a module-scope binding that isn't shadowed by a local function scope, those count as M1 mutations—like `_warnings.push(x)`. For plain reassignments (M2), I need to ensure the enclosing function isn't an IIFE and the binding isn't in any guarded list. Looking at the actual cases: writes at module scope don't get flagged since there's no enclosing function. But inside an IIFE like `linkVocabulary`, even though we're in a function context, the IIFE exemption applies, so reassignments like `VOCABULARY_LINKED = true` are correctly skipped.

Now I'm moving on to build the eval harness with fixtures and the run.js file, then set up the workflow and wire together the LANES and CALIBRATIONS. I'm designing run.js to load JSON fixtures from the fixtures directory, dispatch each one by its kind field to the appropriate runner (adjudication, entailment, gate, or quorum-immunity), compare actual versus expected outputs, and aggregate metrics like true positives, false positives, precision, and abstain rates. The script will exit with different codes depending on whether precision is perfect, immunity checks pass, and all fixtures match their expected results.

For adjudication fixtures, I'm building a runner that constructs a scripted llmCall returning predetermined verdicts, calls the adjudicate function with candidates and bundle data, then compares the resulting states against expected states to determine if it shipped a positive (violation found) matching the expected polarity. The entailment fixture structure follows a similar pattern with claims and scripted responses. Now for quorum-immunity fixtures, I'm setting up a task with multiple providers from different LLM families, each returning their own verdict or veto. The runner builds these providers, calls the quorum function with a specified count and deadline, then checks whether the result achieves immunity status and whether any veto was triggered—if a veto fires, the test fails immediately. These immunity tasks don't count as classification positives; they're evaluated separately for their veto behavior.

For precision calculation, I'm treating abstained tasks as neutral rather than false positives. When there are no positive predictions at all, precision is undefined, so I'm setting it to 1.0 by convention. The brief requires exiting with code 1 if precision drops below 1.0 on the seeded set, but since known-breach fixtures do ship true positives, precision should be well-defined in practice. The abstain rate measures what fraction of classification tasks resulted in the system declining to make a positive claim.

I'm treating any mismatch between actual and expected results as a failure to catch regressions, and I'll document these cases. Now I'm building the test harness with async fixture runners and a main function that collects results, generates a report, and returns the appropriate exit code. I need to write at least twelve fixtures, starting with three known-breach adjudication cases.

For the first fixture, I'm creating a network event artifact—a pre-consent tracker request—that bypasses normal classification and triggers a violation directly. The classifyEvidenceKind function recognizes the network_event type and marks it as observed, which stampBypass converts to a violation state. The candidate survives with the expected violation outcome and counts as a positive result.

The second fixture uses a register_row artifact that similarly bypasses to violation. For the third fixture, I'm testing a text candidate with a quote artifact and a scripted verdict of 'breach'—the adjudicator parses this verdict and maps it to a violation state, which should also produce the expected violation outcome.

Now I'm looking at the fourth fixture, which is a practice-area page with a text candidate containing an evidence quote about defending fraud clients. This one has a 'no_breach' verdict with a disproof mechanism that combines the evidence quote with any absence evidence to form the full evidence text.

The disproof string is 34 characters long, and when I check if its 40-character anchor appears in the lowercased evidence text, it matches — so this correctly passes validation. The fifth fixture is an insufficient verdict case that needs review.

For the sixth fixture, I'm setting up a tempting scenario where the model might try to fabricate a disproof that isn't actually present in the evidence, which should force it to abstain and mark the case as needing review rather than incorrectly clearing it.

Now moving through the entailment test cases: when the premise genuinely entails the hypothesis and the model correctly identifies this, that's a true positive. For neutral and contradiction verdicts, those should fail validation. A malformed response that isn't valid JSON gets rejected at the gate. Finally, I'm checking that the system properly rejects responses citing sources outside the allowed set, which directly tests the source validation rule.

For the quorum-immunity cases, when a task is marked as curated and providers are scripted to veto, the verdict should be immune regardless of the underlying logic. I have 14 fixtures total covering these scenarios.

For the entailment fixtures specifically, the scripted response is what the LLM call returns—for the entailed case, it's an object with the source ID and entailment verdict. The checkEntailment function builds a prompt with the allowed source IDs and source content, then validateResponse checks that response against the schema and allowed sources.

The response cites a source that's in the allowed set, so it passes the first gate, and the verdict is entailment, so ok is true. I'm deciding whether to make the scripted response a JSON object or string—objects are cleaner since validateResponse's parseResponse treats objects with no `.text` property and ok !== false as pre-parsed values. For the garbage case, the scripted response is just a plain string like "the answer is entailment," which validateResponse will try to extract JSON from, and since there's no valid JSON, it becomes unparseable.

For the gate-outofset fixture, I'm crafting a response object with a source_id that's not in the allowed set, so checkSourceIds will reject it and return ok false. I'll provide a minimal schema and test the out-of-set rejection logic.

For gate-quote-drift, I'm setting up a response where the quote is paraphrased text that doesn't match the actual source verbatim — after normalization, the source won't contain the quote, triggering quote_drift and returning ok false.

Now I need to verify that the adjudication fixtures' candidate artifact types actually classify as expected, checking that ARTIFACT_TYPES values like 'network_event' are properly registered.

For the 'breach' text candidate with artifact.type='quote', classifyEvidenceKind maps it to artifactKind='absence' since it's in the TEXT family, and with no declared fields, valid returns true and bypass false, so it flows through as a text candidate.

The adjudicate function handles missing fields like description, evidence_quote, and statutory_citation by defaulting them to empty strings, which is fine since the scripted llmCall ignores the prompt anyway.

For bypass candidates, stampBypass sets state='violation' on a copy of the candidate, and adjudicate returns findings in the same order as the input candidates. When there are no text candidates (bypass-only fixtures), llmCall can be null and adjudicateText simply won't be called.

Defining the result contract for each runner...

TP occurs when we expect a positive and one ships, FP when we don't expect it but ship anyway, and FN when we expect it but don't ship. Precision is calculated as TP divided by TP plus FP, with a default of 1 if there are no false positives. The abstention rate tracks classification tasks that don't produce a result, and I'm counting immunity vetoes separately. The harness fails if precision drops below 1, there are any immunity vetoes, or any task doesn't pass. I'll keep the functions small and import the four shell runners, though adjudicate being async with its dependencies is a consideration. For error handling, I'll wrap each runner in try-catch so that thrown errors set passed to false and shipped to false, preventing errors from being miscounted as false positives. input.content:
# The LLM-eval gate: the blocking precision + abstain-rate harness for every LLM-touched shell
# (PRD tool #9; Constitution Rule 11 + Rule 12 gates 3/4/5). It replays a fixture suite through the
# DETERMINISTIC shells with SCRIPTED providers - no network, no model, no secrets anywhere.
#
# A prompt or model change that lowers precision below 1.0 on the seeded set, fires an immunity veto,
# or regresses a fixture's expectation fails this check.
name: LLM eval

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: llm-eval-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  llm-eval:
    name: LLM precision + abstain-rate harness
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node 24
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 24
          cache: npm

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: LLM eval harness (precision must be 1.0, no immunity veto)
        run: node llm/evals/run.js

      - name: LLM gate + entailment unit tests
        run: node --test llm/entailment.test.js llm/prompts/entailment.test.js llm/evals/run.test.js

### The entailment prompt contract (Gate 3)
`checkEntailment(claims, { llmCall, deadlineMs, now, log })` → `Promise<[{ claim, premise_source_id, verdict: 'entailment'|'neutral'|'contradiction', ok, reason }]>`. Each atomic claim's cited span is the **premise**, the claim is the **hypothesis**. `llm/prompts/entailment.js buildEntailmentPrompt({hypothesis, premise, sourceId})` returns `{system, prompt, schema, allowedSourceIds:[sourceId], sources:{[sourceId]:premise}, labels}`. Every model reply passes `llm/gate.js validateResponse` against a **closed 3-value enum** schema (`required:['source_id','verdict']`); `source_id` is retrieval-gated to the single premise id (Gate 1 composes on top, so a fabricated citation abstains). `ok:true` **only** when the gate-validated verdict is exactly `entailment`; neutral/contradiction/gate-reject/timeout/throw/unparseable → `ok:false`, verdict defaults to the conservative `neutral`. Injected `llmCall`, no network, each call wrapped in `router.withDeadline` (Rule 9). Premise is DOC-delimited and injection-sanitised (C-134); prompt names no law/fine/regulator (Rule 2).

### One-line adjudicator wiring for Gate 3 (for you — I did **not** touch `breach/adjudicator/`)
In `adjudicate.js`, after a text candidate is ruled `breach` and before `stampFromVerdict` writes `violation`:
```
const [e] = await checkEntailment([{ claim: f.description, premise_source_id: f.evidence_source_id || f.evidence_url, premise: f.evidence_quote }], { llmCall: opts.llmCall, deadlineMs: remaining });
if (!e || !e.ok) demote to needs_review (adjudication_reason: 'nli:' + (e && e.verdict));
```
The premise must be the exact verbatim span the quote-match verifier already string-matched to the corpus, so Gate 2 feeds Gate 3.

### no-module-state — detection rule + tree result
acorn AST over `evidence/ breach/ llm/ facts/`. Flags a module-scope binding **mutated from inside a function**: accumulators (`++`/`--`/arithmetic-compound `+=`/mutating methods `.push/.add/.set/…`/property-write on a module object), always flagged; and unguarded plain reassignment of a module-scope `let`/`var`, **except** the benign singletons the constitution allows — module-init IIFE writes, guarded write-once memoisation (`if (x) return x; x = …`), idempotent self-refs (`x = x || v`), require caches and frozen consts. It flags the **mutation**, not the declaration, because every top-level `let`/`var` in the current tree (`facts/sector.js` `_vocabCache`/`_compileDetectGlobalFn`, `facts/capabilities.js` `VOCABULARY_LINKED`/`VOCABULARY_FAILURE`, `evidence/registers/lib/name-match.js` suffix caches, `registers.js` `canonicalSector`) is exactly one of those exempt classes — flagging declarations would falsely break the tree. **On the real tree: 0 findings** (46 files). Fixture `p3-gate-module-state.js` seeds the `_WARN`/`_SWARN`/`_lastDomain` C-153 disease (3 mutations), all caught; the guarded `_built` cache correctly spared.
